documents.source: Aisha al-Taymuriyya, Egyptian woman poet and novelist, essay titled “Family Reform Comes Only Through the Education of Girls,” article published in Al-Adab, a conservative Egyptian newspaper, 1889
documents.content:
“It is the duty of the male establishment to concern itself with the education of girls and the refinement of
families. Take the example of a man in the following situation—his educated wife loves him, comforts him,
and expertly manages his household. Despite this, he works to hide her abilities from the rest of society, and
fears that by announcing her worth it will be said that she is an educated woman, which will disrupt his
peaceful life.
How astounding that our civilization is obsessed with decorating young women in jewelry, and exaggerating
their beauty. But this practice casts these girls into a pit of evil; they have earned nothing from these trinkets
but vanity. If male authorities strove for the improvement and progress of these girls’ behavior through their
education, these women would then find themselves adorned with necklaces strung with pearls of wisdom.”

documents.source: Zaynab Fawwaz, Lebanese woman writer, “Fair and Equal Treatment,” essay published in an Egyptian newspaper, 1891 “The fact is that we have not seen any law coming from Islam ruling that women are to be prohibited from involvement in the occupations of men
documents.content:
A woman is just as much a human being as a man is, with complete
mental faculties and abilities. So many women have ruled over men, participated in government, determined
statutes of law and behavior, recruited soldiers, gone forth into battle, and carried out wars—like the many
queens who have preceded us and ran their kingdoms well.
Regarding the poor women in Cairo or Alexandria, most of them pursue work just like men. Among these
women are merchants, craft workers, and those earning their livelihood with male laborers in construction
and in other professions. We find the markets are full of women capable of competing with men in business
and many other activities. The intelligent person who examines the world’s affairs finds the two sexes equal.”

documents.source: Doria Shafik, Egyptian woman feminist, unpublished memoir written in 1960, describing an event from her childhood that occurred circa 1915 “An agitated atmosphere reigned in our house that Monday
documents.content:
The cause of this gloom was the news that a
family friend had just learned that her husband had taken a second wife, because she had given birth to twin
daughters but failed to produce a male heir. Now faced with a second wife in her household she shouted, ‘I
want a divorce!’
With that shout an icy silence fell over the room. I was astonished both by the woman’s violent reaction as
well as the energetic opposition to her decision by the women around her. Grandmother, whose attachment to
the past could not be severed so easily, led the campaign. ‘I don’t understand you women of today insisting
on exclusiveness. By taking a second wife the husband reduces the burden on the first woman. When my
husband took another wife, I was pleased to have a new friend. And even when he took a third wife we all
banded together against him, the common enemy. Never pronounce the word divorce! It brings dishonor for
you and your daughters, who will never see a suitor if you act this way,’ concluded my grandmother.
My grandmother’s words seemed to act as a whip over the other women as the thought of their daughters
being old maids sank in and they all rallied around her. ‘Forget this idea,’ my mother counseled, ‘you must
fight to regain your husband’s love.’”

documents.source: “A Feminist Demonstration against British rule in Egypt,” cover illustration of the French illustrated news magazine Le Petit Journal, 1922 Private Collection Stefano Bianchetti / Bridgeman Images
documents.content:
A caption in French below the image reads, “While in some streets of the city riots broke out, the women
of Cairo organized in certain quarters into important processions.” A group of British soldiers on
horseback is shown in the background (on the left).

documents.source: Anonymous Turkish woman, interview on the popularity of ballroom dancing, published in the journal Resimli Ay (The Illustrated Monthly), Istanbul, Turkey, 1924
documents.content:
“When, a few years ago, the dance craze came through I was very hesitant to take it up. My friends would
rave about dancing, but I couldn’t help thinking that dancing in a man’s arms was ridiculous, when just a few
years earlier we would not have even been able to sit next to them.
But my social environment gradually led me to embrace dancing. Those who had already taken it up kept
accusing me of stupidity for my refusal to join them. So, even though I was naturally inclined to be
conservative, I decided that one must accept these new trends to be modern, or at least to avoid being labeled.
In order to be able to go to a party organized by one of my friends, I took dance lessons from a Greek lady
for three months.
The dance party was amazing, but my husband’s behavior annoyed me to no end. His glances suggested that
he did not like seeing me mingle with strange men. That night I danced until morning. I paid no attention to
the clumsiness of my feet or to my shyness. I was just excited to be able to punish my husband for his rude
behavior.”

documents.source: Eastern Women’s Congress, an international summit of women from Muslim countries held in Tehran, Iran, under the direction of Princess Ashraf Pahlavi, daughter of the Shah of Iran, resolution, 1932
documents.content:
The Congress supports the following:
1. The right of Eastern women to vote and, when qualified, to be elected to office.
2. Compulsory education for girls and literacy courses for adult women.
3. Equal salaries to men and women in similar employment.
4. Kindergartens, playgrounds, and educational cinemas provided by the government.
5. Measures to improve the morals of Eastern men.
6. Polygamy must be forbidden.
7. Alcohol and narcotics should be prohibited.
8. Hiring female police officers to examine sanitary conditions and improve the lives of mothers and
children.

documents.source: Zaynab al-Ghazali, Egyptian woman political activist, leader of the Muslim Women’s Association, a group affiliated with the Islamist organization The Muslim Brotherhood,1 memoir published in 1986, recalling events in the 1940s
documents.content:
“This was the pact that I made with my husband before entering into our marriage:
‘There is something in my life that you need to know about before you become my husband. I have sworn an
oath of loyalty to Hassan al-Banna [the founder of the Muslim Brotherhood] and have pledged to live my life
in God’s path and to call others to Islam. I cannot ask you today to share this calling with me, but it is my
right to demand that you not block me from pursuing it. When the day comes that my calling leads me to join
the ranks of the fighters for the establishment of the Islamic state, do not ask me what I am doing. And if ever
the welfare of our marriage conflicts with God’s calling, then know that the marriage will end and the calling
will become my whole life. I know that, as husband, it is your right to command me and it is my duty to obey
you, but know ahead of time that God is greater than us and our marriage and that His calling must be dearer
to ourselves than we are to each other.’”
1 a group founded in 1928 that advocated for greater influence of Islam in all aspects of Egyptian society
END OF DOCUMENTS FOR QUESTION 1

parts.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which Muslim women in the Middle East challenged social norms in the period circa
1850–1950.
parts.scoring_notes: Full rubric: Thesis (1), Contextualization (1), Evidence from Documents (2), Evidence Beyond Documents (1), Sourcing (1), Complexity (1). Total: 7 points.

scoring_points.description: Thesis: Historically defensible thesis/claim about the extent to which women in the Middle East challenged social norms in the period circa 1850 to 1950.

required_elements: Position on the extent of women's challenge to social norms
Line of reasoning or analytic categories
alternatives.correct_example: Many women tried to challenge Islamic social norms through appeals to religious scripture and authority.

required_elements: Recognizes both reformist and traditionalist female responses
alternatives.correct_example: Even as some Muslim women fought for legal and political equality, many other women chose to live within the established patriarchal gender norms of their society.

required_elements: Thesis with analytic categories (education, employment, political participation)
alternatives.correct_example: Some Muslim women successfully challenged the many social norms that restricted their rights, including their access to education and employment, legal obstacles to equality with men, and political participation.

wrong_examples: Muslim women often questioned their position in society — defensible but no line of reasoning.
Unlike feminists in the West, women in Muslim societies did not have opportunities to express their political views — establishes a line of reasoning but not historically defensible.
Western imperialism often held back the progress of women in Muslim societies — does not focus on the topic of the prompt (women challenging social norms).
common_traps: Writing a thesis that does not actually evaluate extent — must take a position.
A thesis that focuses on Western influence rather than on women's own challenges to norms is off-topic.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Responds to the prompt with a historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning. The thesis must consist of one or more sentences located in one place, either in the introduction or the conclusion (which may not be limited to the first or last paragraphs). The thesis or claim must identify a relevant development(s) in the period.

sample_responses.response: Many women tried to challenge Islamic social norms through appeals to religious scripture and authority.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — establishes a line of reasoning evaluating the topic (women using religious authority to challenge norms).

sample_responses.response: Even as some Muslim women fought for legal and political equality, many other women chose to live within the established patriarchal gender norms of their society.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — establishes a line of reasoning that recognizes both challenge and continuity, taking a nuanced position on extent.

sample_responses.response: Some Muslim women successfully challenged the many social norms that restricted their rights, including their access to education and employment, legal obstacles to equality with men, and political participation.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — establishes a line of reasoning with analytic categories (education, employment, legal, political) for body paragraphs.

sample_responses.response: Many Islamic women challenged male authority in the Middle East.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — minimally acceptable thesis/claim. Establishes a defensible position even though brief.

sample_responses.response: Muslim women often questioned their position in society.
sample_responses.commentary: Defensible claim but does not establish a line of reasoning.

scoring_points.description: Contextualization: Accurately describe a context relevant to women challenging social norms in the period circa 1850 to 1950.

required_elements: Broader context relevant to women's challenges (Enlightenment, feminism, reform programs, imperialism, world wars)
More than a phrase or reference
alternatives.correct_example: When governments in the Middle East launched reform programs in the nineteenth century this often raised the question of whether women's roles in Muslim society should also be reformed and modernized.

required_elements: Comparative context (Western women's similar struggles)
alternatives.correct_example: Like Western women, women in the Middle East had to overcome a lot of challenges in their struggle against patriarchal social norms.

wrong_examples: During the 19th and early 20th centuries, Egypt was ruled by a dynasty established by Muhammad Ali, an Ottoman soldier of Albanian background — context not relevant to topic of women challenging norms.
Sharia law was still applied in many parts of the Middle East — passing phrase only.
Vague references to 'this was an important period' without development.
common_traps: Context must be relevant to women's challenges to norms — not just any background fact about the Middle East.

SG-acceptable contexts: Enlightenment natural rights, feminism/women's suffrage, Ottoman collapse, Industrial Revolution, non-Western modernization, European imperialism in MENA, World Wars I/II, Cold War onset.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Describes a broader historical context relevant to the prompt. Context must relate the topic of the prompt to broader historical events, developments, or processes that occur before, during, or continue after the time frame of the question. To earn this point, the context provided must be more than a phrase or reference.

sample_responses.response: When governments in the Middle East launched reform programs in the nineteenth century this often raised the question of whether women's roles in Muslim society should also be reformed and modernized.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — describes the broader context of state-led modernization and explicitly connects it to the issue of women's role reform.

sample_responses.response: Like Western women, women in the Middle East had to overcome a lot of challenges in their struggle against patriarchal social norms.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — minimally acceptable contextualization. Establishes a comparative context (Western feminist parallels).

sample_responses.response: During the 19th and early 20th centuries, Egypt was ruled by a dynasty established by Muhammad Ali, an Ottoman soldier of Albanian background.
sample_responses.commentary: Not relevant to the topic of women challenging social norms — biographical context about rulers, not about gender reform.

sample_responses.response: Sharia law was still applied in many parts of the Middle East.
sample_responses.commentary: Passing phrase — does not develop the context or connect it to women's challenges to social norms.

scoring_points.description: Evidence from Documents (1 point): Accurately describe content from at least three documents to address the topic of women challenging social norms.

required_elements: Accurate description (not just quotation) of content from at least three documents
Each description must address the topic of women challenging norms
alternatives.correct_example: Document 4's magazine cover displays women protesting in the streets of Cairo holding up flags.

wrong_examples: Quoting documents without describing their content.
Misinterpreting a document's content (e.g., reading Document 3 as supporting feminism when it shows older generation defending polygamy).
common_traps: Quote, without an accompanying description, fails to earn this point.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Uses the content of at least three documents to address the topic of the prompt. Accurately describe — rather than simply quote — the content from at least three of the documents to address the topic of women challenging social norms in the period circa 1850–1950.

sample_responses.response: The magazine cover [Document 4] displays women protesting in the streets of Cairo holding up flags.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — accurately describes Document 4's content (street protest by Egyptian women) and addresses the topic of women challenging norms via political activism.

sample_responses.response: Document 1 (al-Taymuriyya) argues that male authorities should support girls' education to refine families. Document 2 (Fawwaz) argues that no Islamic law prohibits women from male occupations and cites historical female rulers and working-class women in Cairo. Document 6 (Eastern Women's Congress) lists feminist demands including suffrage, equal pay, compulsory girls' education, and a polygamy ban.
sample_responses.commentary: Accurately describes content from three documents, each addressing how women in different contexts challenged social norms.

sample_responses.response: The documents show that women had problems.
sample_responses.commentary: Vague — does not describe content from three specific documents.

scoring_points.description: Evidence from Documents (2nd point): Support an argument using at least six documents.

required_elements: At least six documents used to support an argument
Evidence explicitly connected to the argument
alternatives.correct_example: Doria Shafik's account [Document 3] shows that many Egyptian women continued to observe the expected code of behavior because they believed that it was the only way for a woman to have a secure and honorable life. This reveals that some women did not want to change social norms.

required_elements: Use of Documents 4 (Cairo march) for political activism argument
alternatives.correct_example: The image [Document 4] demonstrates that conservative Muslim women were willing and able to organize politically and engage in political activism which provides a visual example of social norms being challenged as they are marching alongside the men as equals.

wrong_examples: Using six documents but merely listing without connecting to an argument.
common_traps: The six documents do not have to support a single argument — they can be used across sub-arguments or counterarguments.
Each document must be used argumentatively, not merely cited.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Supports an argument in response to the prompt using at least six documents. The six documents do not have to be used in support of a single argument — they can be used across sub-arguments or to address counterarguments.

sample_responses.response: Doria Shafik's account [Document 3] shows that many Egyptian women continued to observe the expected code of behavior because they believed that it was the only way for a woman to have a secure and honorable life. This reveals that some women did not want to change social norms.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — connects Document 3 to a counterargument about women's adherence to traditional norms (extent of challenge was uneven).

sample_responses.response: The image [Document 4] demonstrates that conservative Muslim women were willing and able to organize politically and engage in political activism which provides a visual example of social norms being challenged as they are marching alongside the men as equals.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — connects Document 4 to an argument about women challenging norms through political activism.

sample_responses.response: Documents 1, 2, and 6 (al-Taymuriyya on education, Fawwaz on workforce equality, Eastern Women's Congress on suffrage) show direct intellectual challenges to gender norms; Documents 4 and 7 (Cairo march, al-Ghazali's marriage pact) show political and religious activism; Document 5 (Turkish dance interview) shows everyday social challenges; Document 3 (Shafik on grandmother's defense of polygamy) provides counterargument complexity. Together, these six-plus documents support an argument that Muslim women's challenges to social norms in 1850–1950 were widespread but uneven across class, generation, and ideology.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses all seven documents argumentatively across sub-arguments — exceeds the six-document threshold.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Beyond Documents (1 point): At least one specific piece of historical evidence beyond the documents relevant to the argument about Muslim women challenging social norms.

required_elements: Specific (not vague) piece of historical evidence not in the documents
Must be different from the contextualization evidence
alternatives.correct_example: Mustafa Kemal Atatürk's determination to eradicate all traces of the legacy of the Ottoman empire led to a number of radical changes for Turkish women, including the ban on women wearing the veil.

required_elements: Specific named Enlightenment/feminist writer or movement
alternatives.correct_example: The spread of Enlightenment or feminist ideas of gender equality, such as those expressed by Condorcet and Mary Wollstonecraft influenced some women in the Middle East to advocate for better access to education and legal equality.

wrong_examples: Repeating something from the prompt or a document.
A vague phrase like 'there was feminism' without naming an event, person, or policy.
Using the same evidence as contextualization (must be different).
common_traps: Confusing evidence with contextualization — evidence must be more specific.

SG-acceptable evidence: Condorcet/de Gouges/Wollstonecraft writings; Seneca Falls; British suffragettes; Tanzimat or Hamidian reforms; Atatürk's reforms; Egyptian reforms under Muhammad Ali's successors; Egyptian debt crisis; Suez Canal; Ottoman capitulations; British colonial control over Egypt 1882; League of Nations Mandates (Syria, Iraq, Lebanon, Jordan); Urabi Revolt; French control over Syria; Pahlavi dynasty; Reza Shah and Mohammad Reza Shah's pro-Western reforms.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Uses at least one additional piece of specific historical evidence (beyond that found in the documents) relevant to an argument about the prompt. The evidence must be different from the evidence used to earn the point for contextualization, must be more than a phrase or reference, and must be relevant to an argument about the extent to which women in the Middle East challenged social norms in the period circa 1850 to 1950.

sample_responses.response: Mustafa Kemal Atatürk's determination to eradicate all traces of the legacy of the Ottoman empire led to a number of radical changes for Turkish women, including the ban on women wearing the veil.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — specific named historical evidence (Atatürk's reforms, veil ban) not in the documents, connected to an argument about state-driven changes that complemented women's own challenges to norms.

sample_responses.response: The spread of Enlightenment or feminist ideas of gender equality, such as those expressed by Condorcet and Mary Wollstonecraft influenced some women in the Middle East to advocate for better access to education and legal equality.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — names specific Enlightenment thinkers (Condorcet, Wollstonecraft) outside the documents and connects to an argument about Middle Eastern women's challenges to norms.

sample_responses.response: There were many feminist movements at this time.
sample_responses.commentary: Too vague — no specific named evidence.

scoring_points.description: Sourcing (1 point): For at least three documents, explain how/why POV, purpose, historical situation, or audience is relevant to an argument.

required_elements: Explanation (not merely identification) of POV, purpose, situation, or audience
Must cover at least three documents
Each must be connected to an argument
alternatives.correct_example: Although the author [of Document 5] states that she comes from a conservative background, she comes to realize that challenging social norms by embracing dancing can be a liberating experience.

wrong_examples: Document 4 shows an image that was produced for a French audience — identifies audience without explaining how/why it matters.
Document 1 says that women's real beauty comes from knowledge — summarizes content, not sourcing.
common_traps: Identifying POV/purpose/situation/audience without explaining HOW/WHY it matters.
Summarizing the content of a document and calling it sourcing.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For at least three documents, explains how or why the document's point of view, purpose, historical situation, and/or audience is relevant to an argument. Must explain how or why — rather than simply identifying — the POV, purpose, historical situation, or audience is relevant to an argument that addresses the prompt for each of the three documents sourced.

sample_responses.response: Although the author [of Document 5] states that she comes from a conservative background, she comes to realize that challenging social norms by embracing dancing can be a liberating experience.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — connects the POV of the Turkish anonymous author to an argument about women's attitudes toward rapidly changing gender norms in 1920s Turkey.

sample_responses.response: Writing in her memoirs decades later, the author's purpose [in Document 7] may have been to emphasize her lifelong dedication to the Muslim Brotherhood and the 'calling' of political Islam.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — connects the purpose of Document 7 (al-Ghazali's retrospective memoir) to an argument about women challenging social norms by embracing political Islamist activism.

sample_responses.response: Aisha al-Taymuriyya's essay [Document 1] was written at a time when Egyptian society, like many other predominantly Muslim societies, was trying to balance social changes arising from modernization reforms against traditional values.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — connects the historical situation of Document 1 to an argument about social change in the 19th-century Middle East and women's ability to challenge norms.

sample_responses.response: Because her audience was mostly Muslim, she [Fawwaz, Document 2] makes her case for economic equality using the teachings of Islam.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — connects the intended audience of Document 2 to an argument about how Middle Eastern women framed their challenges to norms in religiously legible terms.

sample_responses.response: Document 4 shows an image that was produced for a French audience.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the audience without explaining how or why it is relevant to an argument.

scoring_points.description: Complexity (1 point): Demonstrate a complex understanding of the extent to which Muslim women in the Middle East challenged social norms in 1850–1950.

required_elements: Sustained complex argumentation

Ways to earn: nuance through multiple variables; similarity AND difference, or continuity AND change, or multiple causes, or causes AND effects; cross-period/cross-space connections; corroboration of multiple perspectives; qualification by diverse views/evidence
alternatives.correct_example: A response may explore the way in which variables other than gender (age, class, extent of religious devotion, marital status, etc.) affected Muslim Middle Eastern women's ability or willingness to challenge established social norms during this period. A response may argue that Document 2 shows that class divisions made some women much more likely to challenge gender norms by engaging in economic activities usually seen as 'male,' that Document 3 demonstrates a generational divide in women's willingness to challenge gender norms, and that Documents 5 and 7 indicate that being married did not prevent Muslim Middle Eastern women from being able to challenge established social norms.

required_elements: Cross-period connection (women's activism then vs. today)
alternatives.correct_example: A response may outline the similarities and differences between the issues that prompted women to challenge social norms in the period of the prompt with issues that drive women's social and political activism in the Muslim Middle East today.

wrong_examples: In conclusion, this period was complex — single closing sentence.
Listing multiple women without connecting them analytically.
common_traps: Complexity must be part of the argument, not merely a phrase.

Best paths per SG: analyzing multiple variables (age/class/devotion/marital status); cross-period connections (then vs. today); corroboration showing the women didn't fall into neat dichotomous categories (religious vs. secular, conservative vs. liberal, pro- vs. anti-Western).
scoring_points.official_rubric: Demonstrates a complex understanding of the historical development that is the focus of the prompt, using evidence to corroborate, qualify, or modify an argument that addresses the question. This demonstration of complex understanding must be part of the argument, not merely a phrase or reference.

sample_responses.response: Variables other than gender (age, class, extent of religious devotion, marital status) affected Muslim Middle Eastern women's ability or willingness to challenge established social norms. Document 2 shows that class divisions made some women much more likely to challenge gender norms by engaging in economic activities usually seen as 'male,' Document 3 demonstrates a generational divide in women's willingness to challenge gender norms, and Documents 5 and 7 indicate that being married did not prevent Muslim Middle Eastern women from being able to challenge established social norms.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — explains nuance by analyzing multiple variables (class, generation, marital status) sustained across the argument with specific document references.

sample_responses.response: A response may outline the similarities and differences between the issues that prompted women to challenge social norms in the period of the prompt with issues that drive women's social and political activism in the Muslim Middle East today.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — explains relevant and insightful connections across time periods (then vs. now) about Middle Eastern women's activism.

sample_responses.response: Middle Eastern Muslim women's challenges to established social norms during this period, as illustrated in the documents, do not follow neatly into dichotomous categories, such as religious vs. secular, conservative vs. liberal, or pro-Western vs. anti-Western. While several documents argue for social change based on the belief in the equality between men and women, Document 7, Document 4, and, to a lesser extent, Document 5, demonstrate that conservative and religious women also embraced political activism and/or were willing to challenge social norms to advance their goals.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — corroborates multiple perspectives by showing the false dichotomies in characterizing women's activism (religious women like al-Ghazali also challenged norms).

sample_responses.response: In conclusion, this period was complex.
sample_responses.commentary: Single closing sentence — does not demonstrate sustained complexity as part of the argument.
